R-454B (GWP 466) and R-32 (GWP 675) both fall below this threshold and are approved. R-410A (GWP 2088), R-22 (GWP 1810), R-404A (GWP 3922), and R-507A (GWP 3985) all exceed 700 GWP and cannot be used in new equipment.

question: R-32 has a GWP of 675. Is it compliant for use in new high-pressure HVAC equipment after January 1, 2026?
options: No — R-32 is an A2L refrigerant and all A2L refrigerants are banned in new equipment
No — R-32's GWP exceeds 700 and therefore cannot be used in new equipment
Yes — R-32's GWP of 675 is below the 700 threshold, making it compliant for new equipment
Yes — but only in commercial applications, not residential
answer_text: Yes — R-32's GWP of 675 is below the 700 threshold, making it compliant for new equipment
explanation: R-32 has a GWP of 675, which is below the AIM Act's January 1, 2026 threshold of GWP >700 for new equipment. Despite being classified A2L (lower flammability), its GWP classification is not the limiting factor here. The 700 GWP limit applies to refrigerant GWP, not flammability class, per 40 CFR §84.

question: A technician uses a non-spark-free recovery machine on an R-32 system. What is the most serious concern?
options: The recovery machine will not achieve the required recovery efficiency for R-32
The refrigerant may ignite because internal sparks in non-rated equipment can ignite A2L vapors
The recovery machine oil will become contaminated with R-32 and require immediate replacement
This only violates manufacturer warranty — there is no safety or regulatory concern
answer_text: The refrigerant may ignite because internal sparks in non-rated equipment can ignite A2L vapors
explanation: R-32 is classified A2L under ASHRAE Standard 34, meaning it has lower flammability with a burning velocity ≤10 cm/s. Non-spark-free equipment can generate internal electrical sparks that may ignite A2L vapors if concentrations reach flammable limits. ASHRAE Standard 15-2022 requires spark-free (ignition-protected) equipment for servicing A2L systems.

question: Which of the following tools must be spark-free when servicing an R-454B system?
options: Only the refrigerant recovery cylinder needs to be spark-free rated
Only manifold gauge sets need to be spark-free rated for A2L
Recovery machines, vacuum pumps, and electronic leak detectors must all be spark-free rated
No special spark-free tools are required because R-454B is not flammable enough to ignite
answer_text: Recovery machines, vacuum pumps, and electronic leak detectors must all be spark-free rated
explanation: ASHRAE Standard 15-2022 requires that electrical service equipment used with A2L refrigerants be spark-free (ignition-protected). This includes recovery machines, vacuum pumps, and electronic leak detectors — all of which contain electric motors or circuits that can generate sparks. Manifold gauge sets (mechanical) and hand tools generally do not require special rating.

question: Which safety precautions apply to working with R-454B (A2L) that do NOT apply when working with R-410A (A1)?
options: Wearing a full-face respirator and chemical-resistant gloves at all times
Using spark-free service equipment and avoiding ignition sources near the refrigerant
Evacuating all building occupants and posting flammable gas warning signs before service
Obtaining a special flammable refrigerant permit from the local fire marshal
answer_text: Using spark-free service equipment and avoiding ignition sources near the refrigerant
explanation: R-454B (A2L) has lower flammability unlike R-410A (A1, non-flammable). The key additional precaution is using spark-free equipment and avoiding ignition sources per ASHRAE Standard 15-2022. Standard PPE sufficient for R-410A remains adequate for A2L — no respirator upgrade, fire marshal permit, or mandatory occupant evacuation is required for normal service of A2L systems.

subtopic: Evacuation Requirements — Size and Date Matrix
question: A technician is servicing a 250-pound R-454B high-pressure system manufactured in 2023. What is the required evacuation level before opening the system for repair?
options: 0 psig
25 in. Hg vacuum
10 in. Hg vacuum
15 in. Hg vacuum
answer_text: 10 in. Hg vacuum
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.158, high-pressure systems with more than 200 lbs of refrigerant manufactured after November 15, 1993 must be evacuated to 10 in. Hg vacuum. This 2023 system exceeds 200 lbs and was manufactured post-1993, so the 10 in. Hg standard applies. The 25 in. Hg level applies to low-pressure (centrifugal chiller) systems.

subtopic: Evacuation Requirements — Size and Date Matrix
question: A technician is recovering refrigerant from a large high-pressure system manufactured in 1988 that contains 300 lbs of refrigerant. What is the required evacuation level?
options: 10 in. Hg vacuum
25 in. Hg vacuum
0 psig
29.9 in. Hg vacuum
answer_text: 0 psig
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.158(b), high-pressure systems manufactured before November 15, 1993 need only be evacuated to 0 psig (atmospheric pressure) regardless of system size. This 1988 system qualifies for the 0 psig standard. The more stringent 10 in. Hg level applies to systems manufactured after November 15, 1993. The 25 in. Hg level is for low-pressure centrifugal chillers.

subtopic: Evacuation Requirements — Size and Date Matrix
question: What instrument is used to measure the vacuum level during system evacuation to verify compliance with EPA requirements?
options: A manifold gauge set reading in psig
A calibrated electronic vacuum gauge (micron gauge or thermocouple gauge) reading in inches of mercury or microns
A refrigerant scale that measures weight loss during evacuation
A clamp-type ammeter measuring vacuum pump motor current
answer_text: A calibrated electronic vacuum gauge (micron gauge or thermocouple gauge) reading in inches of mercury or microns
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.158, technicians must use an accurate vacuum gauge to verify evacuation levels. Electronic micron gauges or thermocouple vacuum gauges measure in inches of mercury or microns and are required for accurate verification. Standard manifold gauge sets are not precise enough at low vacuum levels to confirm EPA-required evacuation depths.

subtopic: R-454B and R-32 as A2L Refrigerants
question: Compared to R-410A, how does R-454B's environmental impact differ?
options: R-454B has a higher GWP than R-410A and is therefore more environmentally damaging
R-454B and R-410A have identical GWP values but different ozone depletion potentials
R-454B has a GWP of 466, significantly lower than R-410A's GWP of 2088, reducing its climate impact
R-454B is ozone-depleting while R-410A is not, making R-410A the better environmental choice
answer_text: R-454B has a GWP of 466, significantly lower than R-410A's GWP of 2088, reducing its climate impact
explanation: R-454B (GWP 466) represents approximately a 78% reduction in global warming potential compared to R-410A (GWP 2088). Neither refrigerant depletes the ozone layer. This significant GWP reduction is why R-454B was selected as a primary R-410A replacement under the AIM Act's GWP >700 equipment restriction effective January 1, 2026.

question: Does working with R-454B (A2L) require different personal protective equipment (PPE) compared to R-410A (A1)?
options: Yes — A2L refrigerants require flame-resistant clothing and a full-face respirator
Yes — technicians must wear chemical-resistant gloves rated for flammable gases when handling A2L
No — standard PPE used for R-410A service is adequate for R-454B; no upgraded PPE is required
Yes — technicians must wear a self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) rated for flammable atmospheres
answer_text: No — standard PPE used for R-410A service is adequate for R-454B; no upgraded PPE is required
explanation: A2L refrigerants like R-454B have lower flammability (ASHRAE A2L classification), but standard PPE used for R-410A service remains adequate. The primary additional precaution for A2L is spark-free equipment, not upgraded PPE. ASHRAE Standard 15-2022 does not mandate flame-resistant clothing, SCBA, or special gloves for routine A2L service — the key change is equipment, not PPE.

question: A building owner purchased a new packaged rooftop unit containing R-410A that was manufactured in December 2025. Can this unit legally be installed in February 2026?
options: Yes — the unit was manufactured before January 1, 2026, so it may still be installed
No — any installation of equipment containing R-410A is prohibited after January 1, 2026, regardless of manufacture date
Yes — commercial rooftop units are exempt from the GWP >700 ban until 2028
No — R-410A was fully banned including all existing stockpiles starting January 1, 2026
answer_text: Yes — the unit was manufactured before January 1, 2026, so it may still be installed
explanation: The AIM Act's GWP >700 restriction under 40 CFR §84 targets the manufacture and sale of NEW equipment, with an effective date of January 1, 2026. Equipment manufactured before this date may generally still be sold and installed — the restriction applies to manufacturing, not to all subsequent installations of pre-existing inventory. Stockpiled pre-2026 units are not automatically prohibited from installation.

question: A technician's only vacuum pump is not rated for A2L refrigerants. The technician needs to service an R-32 mini-split system. What is the correct action?
options: Proceed with the non-rated pump because R-32 does not have enough flammability to pose any real risk
Use the non-rated pump only if the service area is well-ventilated
Obtain an A2L-rated spark-free vacuum pump before performing evacuation on the R-32 system
Ask the customer to sign a liability waiver, then use the standard vacuum pump
answer_text: Obtain an A2L-rated spark-free vacuum pump before performing evacuation on the R-32 system
explanation: ASHRAE Standard 15-2022 requires spark-free equipment for A2L refrigerant service. Using non-rated equipment on an R-32 system creates an ignition risk and violates safety standards regardless of ventilation. Ventilation reduces risk but does not substitute for spark-free equipment. The technician must obtain proper A2L-rated equipment before performing service.

subtopic: Evacuation Requirements — Size and Date Matrix
question: What evacuation level is required for a centrifugal chiller (low-pressure system) manufactured in 2020 before opening for service?
options: 0 psig
10 in. Hg vacuum
15 in. Hg vacuum
25 in. Hg vacuum
answer_text: 25 in. Hg vacuum
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.158(b), low-pressure appliances (centrifugal chillers using refrigerants like R-123 or R-11) manufactured after November 15, 1993 must be evacuated to 25 in. Hg vacuum. This is the most stringent evacuation requirement because low-pressure systems operate at sub-atmospheric pressures and are at risk of air infiltration. The 10 in. Hg level applies to high-pressure systems.

question: Under the AIM Act, what is the primary regulatory mechanism used to phase down high-GWP refrigerants like R-410A?
options: A complete and immediate ban on all use of high-GWP refrigerants in existing equipment
A phased reduction (phasedown) of allowed production and consumption levels over time
A tax on refrigerants based on their GWP value, making high-GWP refrigerants more expensive
A mandatory retrofit program requiring all existing R-410A equipment to be converted by 2030
answer_text: A phased reduction (phasedown) of allowed production and consumption levels over time
explanation: The American Innovation and Manufacturing (AIM) Act uses a phasedown approach, progressively reducing the allowable production and consumption of high-GWP HFC refrigerants as measured in CO2-equivalent tons. This differs from an outright ban — existing equipment can still be serviced, but new production of virgin high-GWP refrigerants is restricted according to the phasedown schedule per 40 CFR §84.

question: What is the Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) of R-12, the CFC refrigerant used as the reference standard?
explanation: R-12 (CFC-12) has an ODP of 1.0 and serves as the reference compound against which all other refrigerants are measured. ODP quantifies a substance's potential to destroy stratospheric ozone relative to R-12. All other substances are rated on this scale — lower is better. Source: EPA Clean Air Act §605; UNEP Montreal Protocol reference values.

question: Which of the following best describes the difference between ODP and GWP?
options: ODP measures harm to the troposphere; GWP measures harm to the stratosphere
ODP measures potential to destroy stratospheric ozone; GWP measures heat-trapping potential relative to CO2 over 100 years
ODP is measured in parts per million; GWP is measured as a percentage
ODP applies only to HFCs; GWP applies only to CFCs
answer_text: ODP measures potential to destroy stratospheric ozone; GWP measures heat-trapping potential relative to CO2 over 100 years
explanation: ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) quantifies how much a substance can destroy stratospheric ozone relative to R-12 (ODP = 1.0). GWP (Global Warming Potential) measures a substance's heat-trapping ability over a 100-year period relative to CO2 (GWP = 1). These are two separate environmental metrics. Source: 40 CFR Part 82; IPCC AR6.

question: What is the ODP of R-22 (HCFC-22)?
explanation: R-22 (HCFC-22) has an ODP of 0.05, meaning it depletes ozone at 5% the rate of R-12. While significantly lower than R-12's ODP of 1.0, R-22 still contains chlorine atoms that can reach and damage the stratospheric ozone layer. This is why R-22 was phased out under the Montreal Protocol, with production and import ending in 2020 in the U.S. Source: 40 CFR Part 82 Subpart A.

question: R-410A has a GWP of 2088 but an ODP of 0. What does this tell us about R-410A's environmental impact?
options: R-410A is environmentally safe because its ODP is zero
R-410A does not harm the ozone layer but is a potent greenhouse gas
R-410A harms both the ozone layer and contributes to global warming
R-410A's high GWP cancels out any benefit from its zero ODP, making it worse than R-22
answer_text: R-410A does not harm the ozone layer but is a potent greenhouse gas
explanation: R-410A is an HFC blend with ODP = 0 (no chlorine, so no ozone depletion) but a GWP of 2088 (IPCC AR6). This means R-410A solved the ozone crisis concern but contributes significantly to climate change if released. It is regulated under the AIM Act for phasedown due to its high GWP. Source: IPCC AR6; AIM Act (2020); 40 CFR Part 82.
source_ref: IPCC AR6; AIM Act 2020; 40 CFR Part 82

question: Which refrigerant class has BOTH a high ODP AND a high GWP?
options: HFCs (e.g., R-410A, R-134a)
HFOs (e.g., R-1234yf)
CFCs (e.g., R-12, R-11)
Natural refrigerants (e.g., R-744, R-717)
answer_text: CFCs (e.g., R-12, R-11)
explanation: CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) such as R-12 and R-11 have both high ODP (R-12 = 1.0, R-11 = 1.0) and high GWP (R-12 GWP ≈ 10,900; R-11 GWP ≈ 4,750). HFCs have zero ODP but high GWP. HFOs have zero ODP and very low GWP. This dual harm is why CFCs were the first substances phased out under the Montreal Protocol. Source: 40 CFR Part 82; IPCC AR6.

question: A technician is comparing R-454B (GWP 466) and R-410A (GWP 2088) as refrigerant options. Which statement accurately compares these two refrigerants?
options: R-454B has a lower GWP than R-410A and is therefore preferred under low-GWP regulations
R-410A has a lower GWP than R-454B because it is an older refrigerant
Both refrigerants have the same ODP, so GWP does not matter for regulatory compliance
R-454B's GWP of 466 is still above the regulatory threshold, making it unusable in new equipment
answer_text: R-454B has a lower GWP than R-410A and is therefore preferred under low-GWP regulations
explanation: R-454B (per ASHRAE 34-2022) has a GWP of 466, compared to R-410A's GWP of 2088 (IPCC AR6). R-454B represents approximately a 78% GWP reduction over R-410A. Both have ODP = 0. Under the AIM Act phasedown program, lower-GWP alternatives like R-454B and R-32 (GWP 675) are being adopted to replace R-410A in new residential and light commercial HVAC equipment. Source: ASHRAE 34-2022; AIM Act.

question: Which of the following correctly ranks R-22, R-123, and R-12 by ODP from lowest to highest?
options: R-12 (lowest) → R-22 → R-123 (highest)
R-22 (lowest) → R-12 → R-123 (highest)
R-123 (lowest) → R-22 → R-12 (highest)
R-12 (lowest) → R-123 → R-22 (highest)
answer_text: R-123 (lowest) → R-22 → R-12 (highest)
explanation: The ODP values are: R-123 = 0.02, R-22 = 0.05, R-12 = 1.0. R-12 is the reference compound at ODP 1.0. R-22 has a relatively low ODP of 0.05, and R-123 has an even lower ODP of 0.02, making it the least harmful to ozone among these three. All three contain chlorine, distinguishing them from zero-ODP HFCs and HFOs. Source: 40 CFR Part 82; Clean Air Act §605.

question: An HVAC technician is told that HFOs like R-1234yf have zero ODP and a GWP of less than 1. How do HFOs compare to HFCs in terms of both ODP and GWP?
options: HFOs have higher ODP but lower GWP than HFCs
HFOs and HFCs both have zero ODP, but HFOs have much lower GWP than HFCs
HFOs have zero ODP and zero GWP, while HFCs have high ODP and high GWP
HFOs have lower ODP than HFCs, but similar GWP values
answer_text: HFOs and HFCs both have zero ODP, but HFOs have much lower GWP than HFCs
explanation: Both HFCs and HFOs contain no chlorine, giving them ODP = 0. However, HFCs have high GWP values (e.g., R-410A GWP = 2088, R-32 GWP = 675), while HFOs have extremely low GWP (R-1234yf GWP < 1). HFOs degrade rapidly in the atmosphere. This makes HFOs environmentally superior to HFCs on both ozone and climate metrics, which is why HFOs are emerging as next-generation refrigerants. Source: IPCC AR6; ASHRAE 34-2022.

question: In what year was the Montreal Protocol signed?
explanation: The Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer was signed on September 16, 1987, in Montreal, Canada. It entered into force on January 1, 1989. It has since been ratified by virtually every nation in the United Nations, making it one of the most universally ratified environmental treaties in history. Source: UNEP Montreal Protocol; Clean Air Act §605; 40 CFR Part 82.

question: What is the primary goal of the Montreal Protocol?
options: Reduce carbon dioxide emissions to limit global temperature rise to 1.5°C
Phase out ozone-depleting substances (ODSs) such as CFCs and HCFCs
Establish maximum allowable refrigerant charge sizes for HVAC equipment
Require all new refrigerants to have a GWP below 150
answer_text: Phase out ozone-depleting substances (ODSs) such as CFCs and HCFCs
explanation: The Montreal Protocol's primary goal is to protect the stratospheric ozone layer by phasing out the production and consumption of ozone-depleting substances (ODSs), including CFCs, HCFCs, halons, and other chemicals containing chlorine or bromine. It does not directly address GWP or CO2. The Kigali Amendment (2016) extended the treaty to address HFCs. Source: Clean Air Act §605; 40 CFR Part 82.

question: The Montreal Protocol controls which aspect of ozone-depleting substances (ODSs)?
options: Their use in residential air conditioning only
Their production and consumption globally
Their storage and labeling requirements at the point of sale
Their maximum allowable concentration in ambient air
answer_text: Their production and consumption globally
explanation: The Montreal Protocol controls the production and consumption of ODSs at a national level. Each signatory nation agrees to reduce and eventually eliminate the production and importation (consumption) of listed ODSs on a defined schedule. In the U.S., these obligations are implemented through the Clean Air Act §605 and 40 CFR Part 82. Source: Clean Air Act §605; 40 CFR Part 82 Subpart A.
source_ref: Clean Air Act §605; 40 CFR Part 82 Subpart A

question: As a direct result of the Montreal Protocol, CFC production was phased out in the United States by which year?
explanation: Under the Montreal Protocol schedule, the United States phased out production and importation of CFCs (Class I ODSs) by January 1, 1996. HCFC production and importation (Class II ODSs, including R-22) continued on a phasedown schedule, with new R-22 production/import ending in 2020. Source: Clean Air Act §605; 40 CFR Part 82 Subpart A.
source_ref: Clean Air Act §605; 40 CFR Part 82 Subpart A

question: Which of the following is most accurate about R-22 and the Montreal Protocol?
options: R-22 was never regulated under the Montreal Protocol because it is an HCFC, not a CFC
R-22 production and import ended in the U.S. in 2020 as part of the HCFC phasedown schedule
R-22 was phased out in 1996 along with all other chlorinated refrigerants
R-22 is exempt from Montreal Protocol requirements because its ODP is below 0.1
answer_text: R-22 production and import ended in the U.S. in 2020 as part of the HCFC phasedown schedule
explanation: HCFCs like R-22 are Class II ODSs under the Clean Air Act and are regulated under the Montreal Protocol's HCFC phasedown schedule. While CFC production ended in 1996, HCFCs were given a longer timeline. In the U.S., new production and importation of R-22 ended on January 1, 2020. Only recovered, recycled, or reclaimed R-22 may legally be used for servicing existing equipment after that date. Source: Clean Air Act §605; 40 CFR Part 82 Subpart A.
source_ref: Clean Air Act §605; 40 CFR Part 82 Subpart A

question: How does the Kigali Amendment relate to the original Montreal Protocol?
options: The Kigali Amendment replaced the Montreal Protocol in 2016 with stricter CFC regulations
The Kigali Amendment extended the Montreal Protocol to include a phasedown of HFCs due to their high GWP
The Kigali Amendment created new penalties for venting refrigerants in developing nations only
The Kigali Amendment established the AHRI 700 purity standard for reclaimed refrigerants
answer_text: The Kigali Amendment extended the Montreal Protocol to include a phasedown of HFCs due to their high GWP
explanation: The Kigali Amendment, adopted in 2016, extended the Montreal Protocol to address HFCs, which were developed as ODS replacements but have very high GWP values. While HFCs have ODP = 0 (so they don't deplete ozone), their climate impact prompted the amendment. The Kigali Amendment sets a schedule for HFC phasedown in developed and developing nations. The U.S. implemented this domestically through the AIM Act (2020). Source: UNEP Kigali Amendment; AIM Act 2020; 40 CFR Part 82.
source_ref: UNEP Kigali Amendment; AIM Act 2020; 40 CFR Part 82

question: Which of the following refrigerants is classified as a HIGH-pressure refrigerant?
explanation: R-410A is a high-pressure refrigerant with a normal boiling point well below 50°F (approximately -61°F / -52°C), meaning it operates above atmospheric pressure at room temperature. R-11, R-113, and R-123 are all low-pressure refrigerants that boil above 50°F at atmospheric pressure and operate in a vacuum (below atmospheric pressure) at room temperature. Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: What is the defining characteristic of a low-pressure refrigerant?
options: It has a normal boiling point below 50°F and operates above atmospheric pressure at room temperature
It has a normal boiling point above 50°F and operates below atmospheric pressure (in vacuum) at room temperature
It requires a booster compressor to achieve adequate system pressures
It is only used in refrigeration systems, never in air conditioning
answer_text: It has a normal boiling point above 50°F and operates below atmospheric pressure (in vacuum) at room temperature
explanation: Low-pressure refrigerants (e.g., R-11, R-113, R-123) have normal boiling points above 50°F at atmospheric pressure. At room temperature, they exist as vapors at pressures below atmospheric, meaning the system operates in a vacuum. This has important service implications — air can leak INTO a low-pressure system if not carefully managed. Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: Which refrigerants are classified as LOW-pressure refrigerants under EPA regulations?
explanation: R-11, R-113, and R-123 are classified as low-pressure refrigerants because their normal boiling points are above 50°F, causing them to operate below atmospheric pressure at room temperature. R-22, R-134a, R-404A, R-410A, and similar refrigerants are high-pressure types. R-12, R-500, and R-502 are considered medium-pressure refrigerants. Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: A technician is servicing a large centrifugal chiller using R-123. What pressure concern should the technician be aware of?
options: The system may have dangerously high pressures, requiring a high-pressure recovery cylinder
The system operates below atmospheric pressure, so air can leak INTO the system during service
R-123 requires a special high-pressure gauge set calibrated for pressures above 500 psig
The system uses medium pressure, so standard gauge sets are inadequate
answer_text: The system operates below atmospheric pressure, so air can leak INTO the system during service
explanation: R-123 is a low-pressure refrigerant that operates below atmospheric pressure (in a vacuum) at room temperature. This means that if any part of the system is opened or has a leak, air (and moisture) can infiltrate the system. Technicians must be especially vigilant about air contamination in low-pressure systems. A standing vacuum test is often used to check system integrity. Source: 40 CFR §82.152; EPA 608 refrigerant management requirements.

question: Which of the following correctly categorizes R-12, R-22, and R-410A by pressure classification?
options: R-12: low-pressure; R-22: medium-pressure; R-410A: high-pressure
R-12: medium-pressure; R-22: high-pressure; R-410A: high-pressure
R-12: high-pressure; R-22: high-pressure; R-410A: low-pressure
R-12: low-pressure; R-22: high-pressure; R-410A: medium-pressure
answer_text: R-12: medium-pressure; R-22: high-pressure; R-410A: high-pressure
explanation: Under EPA pressure classification: R-12 (CFC-12) is a medium-pressure refrigerant that operates slightly above atmospheric pressure at room temperature. R-22 (HCFC-22) and R-410A are both high-pressure refrigerants, with R-410A operating at significantly higher pressures than R-22 (R-410A can reach 400+ psig on the high side). Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: Why is the distinction between high-pressure and low-pressure refrigerants important for EPA 608 technicians?
options: High-pressure refrigerants require a Type III certification; low-pressure refrigerants require a Type I certification
The pressure classification determines which recovery equipment is required and the leak testing procedures for compliance
Only low-pressure refrigerant systems are subject to EPA venting prohibition rules
High-pressure systems are exempt from refrigerant recovery requirements if under 5 pounds charge
answer_text: The pressure classification determines which recovery equipment is required and the leak testing procedures for compliance
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.152 and EPA Section 608 regulations, pressure classification affects required recovery equipment specifications, leak rate thresholds for different equipment types, and proper service procedures. Low-pressure systems have unique requirements because air infiltration risk is reversed — leaks pull air in rather than pushing refrigerant out. Different recovery machines and techniques are required for low-pressure vs. high-pressure systems. Source: 40 CFR §82.152; 40 CFR §82.156.

question: What purity standard must reclaimed refrigerant meet before it can be resold?
options: ASHRAE 34-2022
SAE J2099
explanation: Reclaimed refrigerant must meet AHRI 700-2016 (Air-Conditioning, Heating, and Refrigeration Institute Standard 700) purity standards before it can be resold or used in equipment owned by a different party. AHRI 700 specifies acceptable levels of moisture, acidity, non-condensable gases, and other contaminants to ensure the refrigerant is equivalent to virgin quality. Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: What is the key difference between RECYCLING and RECLAIMING refrigerant?
options: Recycling occurs at a certified off-site facility; reclaiming occurs on-site using portable equipment
Recycling cleans refrigerant on-site for reuse by the same owner; reclaiming returns refrigerant to virgin purity at a certified facility for resale
Recycling meets AHRI 700 standards; reclaiming only meets minimum EPA purity standards
There is no difference — recycling and reclaiming are interchangeable terms under 40 CFR Part 82
answer_text: Recycling cleans refrigerant on-site for reuse by the same owner; reclaiming returns refrigerant to virgin purity at a certified facility for resale
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.152: Recycling = cleaning refrigerant using oil separation and single or multiple passes through filter-driers on-site; the cleaned refrigerant may be reused only in equipment owned by the same owner. Reclaiming = processing refrigerant to meet AHRI 700-2016 virgin purity standards at a certified off-site reclamation facility; reclaimed refrigerant may be resold. Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: A technician recovers refrigerant from a system and wants to sell it to another contractor. What must happen first?
options: The technician must recycle the refrigerant using on-site equipment and then can sell it
The refrigerant must be sent to a certified reclamation facility to be processed to AHRI 700-2016 standards
The technician must obtain a resale permit from the EPA before transferring the refrigerant
The refrigerant can be sold immediately as long as it was properly recovered using certified equipment
answer_text: The refrigerant must be sent to a certified reclamation facility to be processed to AHRI 700-2016 standards
explanation: Before recovered refrigerant can be resold or transferred to another owner, it must be reclaimed — sent to an EPA-certified reclamation facility where it is processed to meet AHRI 700-2016 purity standards. On-site recycling is insufficient for resale; it only allows reuse within the same owner's equipment. Selling non-reclaimed refrigerant violates 40 CFR Part 82. Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: Which of the following correctly defines the term RECOVER as it relates to refrigerant handling under 40 CFR Part 82?
options: To process refrigerant to AHRI 700-2016 virgin purity standards at a certified facility
To clean refrigerant on-site using oil separation and filter-driers for reuse
To remove refrigerant from a system in any condition and store it in an approved external container
To transfer refrigerant between systems belonging to the same owner without purifying it
answer_text: To remove refrigerant from a system in any condition and store it in an approved external container
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.152, recovery means removing refrigerant from a system and storing it in an external container — regardless of the refrigerant's condition. Recovery does not involve any cleaning or purification. It is the first step in the recover-recycle-reclaim process. Recovery must be performed before opening any system for service. Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: Where must refrigerant reclamation take place to comply with 40 CFR Part 82?
options: On-site at the technician's service vehicle using portable reclaiming equipment
At the equipment owner's facility using EPA-certified in-house reclamation equipment
At an EPA-certified off-site reclamation facility
At any HVAC wholesale distributor that holds a valid refrigerant reseller license
answer_text: At an EPA-certified off-site reclamation facility
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.152, reclamation must be performed at a certified reclamation facility — an off-site facility that has been approved to process refrigerant to AHRI 700-2016 purity standards. On-site cleaning is classified as recycling, not reclaiming. Only properly reclaimed refrigerant that meets AHRI 700-2016 standards may be resold. Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: Which scenario represents a violation of the reclaimed refrigerant requirements under 40 CFR Part 82?
options: A technician sends recovered R-22 to a certified reclaimer and later purchases reclaimed R-22 for use on a different customer's system
A building owner uses recycled refrigerant recovered from one of their own systems in another one of their own systems
A technician recovers R-410A, passes it through an on-site recycling machine, and sells it directly to another contractor
A service company transfers reclaimed R-22 meeting AHRI 700-2016 standards to a customer for use in their equipment
answer_text: A technician recovers R-410A, passes it through an on-site recycling machine, and sells it directly to another contractor
explanation: Selling refrigerant that has only been recycled (cleaned on-site) to another party is a violation of 40 CFR Part 82. Recycled refrigerant may only be reused in equipment owned by the same owner. To legally sell or transfer refrigerant to a different owner, it must first be reclaimed to AHRI 700-2016 standards at an EPA-certified off-site reclamation facility. The other options describe legal activities. Source: 40 CFR §82.152.

question: What is the primary defining characteristic of system-dependent recovery equipment?
options: It has its own built-in compressor and can operate independently
It relies on the refrigeration system's own compressor to assist the recovery process
It is only approved for use on systems containing HFC refrigerants
It must be used with a dedicated recovery cylinder rated above 400 psi
answer_text: It relies on the refrigeration system's own compressor to assist the recovery process
explanation: System-dependent (passive) recovery equipment does not have its own compressor. Instead, it uses the refrigeration system's own compressor to push or pull refrigerant into the recovery cylinder. This distinguishes it from self-contained equipment, which has its own compressor. Per 40 CFR §82.158(b), system-dependent equipment is only approved for use on systems with 15 lbs or less of refrigerant charge.

question: Under EPA regulations, what is the maximum refrigerant charge allowed in a system for system-dependent recovery equipment to be legally used?
options: 5 lbs
10 lbs
15 lbs
50 lbs
answer_text: 15 lbs
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.158(b), system-dependent recovery equipment may only be used on systems containing 15 lbs or less of refrigerant. Systems with more than 15 lbs require self-contained recovery equipment, which has its own compressor and does not rely on the system being serviced.

question: A technician needs to recover refrigerant from a rooftop commercial unit with an 80 lb charge. Which type of recovery equipment is required?
options: System-dependent recovery equipment, because it is faster
Self-contained recovery equipment, because the system charge exceeds 15 lbs
Either type is acceptable as long as the technician is EPA-certified
System-dependent recovery equipment, as long as the system compressor is operational
answer_text: Self-contained recovery equipment, because the system charge exceeds 15 lbs
explanation: 40 CFR §82.158(b) requires self-contained recovery equipment for systems with more than 15 lbs of refrigerant. An 80 lb charge far exceeds this threshold. Self-contained equipment has its own motor-driven compressor and operates independently of the system under service, making it the only compliant choice here.

question: Which of the following best describes self-contained recovery equipment?
options: Equipment that uses the system's own compressor and is limited to systems with less than 15 lbs of refrigerant
Equipment with its own compressor that can recover refrigerant from systems of any size
Equipment that only works with HFC refrigerants and requires a specialized recovery cylinder
Equipment that performs simultaneous recovery and recycling without an external tank
answer_text: Equipment with its own compressor that can recover refrigerant from systems of any size
explanation: Self-contained recovery equipment is equipped with its own motor and compressor, making it independent of the system being serviced. Unlike system-dependent equipment, it is not restricted by system charge size and can be used on systems of any refrigerant charge capacity. This makes it the required choice for systems with more than 15 lbs per 40 CFR §82.158(b).

question: Recovery equipment manufactured after November 15, 1993 must meet which requirement before it can be used for EPA 608 compliance?
options: It must be registered with the EPA directly by the manufacturer
It must be certified by an EPA-approved third-party organization
It must be capable of achieving 0 psi recovery efficiency on all system types
It must be recalibrated annually by a certified HVAC technician
answer_text: It must be certified by an EPA-approved third-party organization
explanation: Under EPA Section 608 regulations, recovery and recycling equipment manufactured after November 15, 1993 must be certified by an EPA-approved third-party testing organization (such as UL or ARI/AHRI) before it may be used for regulatory compliance. This certification verifies that the equipment meets required recovery efficiency standards. Equipment manufactured before that date was grandfathered in without third-party certification.

question: A technician uses system-dependent recovery equipment on a system that contains 20 lbs of R-22. Which statement is correct about this situation?
options: This is compliant because R-22 is an HCFC and has a special exemption
This is a violation because system-dependent equipment is limited to systems with 15 lbs or less
This is compliant as long as the system compressor is running during recovery
This is acceptable if the technician holds a Type II EPA 608 certification
answer_text: This is a violation because system-dependent equipment is limited to systems with 15 lbs or less
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.158(b), system-dependent recovery equipment is approved only for use on systems with a refrigerant charge of 15 lbs or less. A 20 lb system requires self-contained recovery equipment. The type of refrigerant (R-22, R-410A, etc.) or the technician's certification level do not change this equipment requirement — the charge size threshold is what governs equipment type selection.

question: Which scenario would make it impossible to use system-dependent recovery equipment, regardless of system charge size?
options: The system contains an HFC refrigerant such as R-410A
The system compressor is burned out or non-functional
The recovery cylinder is only half full
The outdoor ambient temperature is below 40°F
answer_text: The system compressor is burned out or non-functional
explanation: System-dependent recovery equipment relies on the refrigeration system's own compressor to assist recovery. If the system compressor is non-functional (burned out, seized, or otherwise inoperable), system-dependent equipment cannot perform its function. In this case, self-contained recovery equipment — which has its own compressor — would be required. This is a practical limitation beyond the 15 lb charge threshold stated in 40 CFR §82.158(b).

question: A technician's shop has two recovery machines: Machine A is system-dependent, and Machine B is self-contained. A residential mini-split with a 10 lb charge needs to be serviced. Which machine(s) may legally be used?
options: Only Machine A (system-dependent), because it is more efficient on small systems
Only Machine B (self-contained), because it is always required for EPA compliance
Either Machine A or Machine B may be used, since the charge does not exceed 15 lbs
Neither machine may be used without additional EPA-issued site authorization
answer_text: Either Machine A or Machine B may be used, since the charge does not exceed 15 lbs
explanation: 40 CFR §82.158(b) permits the use of system-dependent recovery equipment on systems with 15 lbs or less of refrigerant charge. A 10 lb system falls within this limit, so both system-dependent (Machine A) and self-contained (Machine B) equipment are legally acceptable options, provided both machines are third-party certified (if manufactured after November 15, 1993). The regulation establishes a ceiling for system-dependent use, not a floor for self-contained use.

question: Which refrigerant leak detection method is considered the most sensitive for pinpointing small leaks?
options: Bubble test using soap solution
Halide torch
Electronic leak detector
Ultraviolet fluorescent dye inspection
answer_text: Electronic leak detector
explanation: Electronic leak detectors are recognized as the most sensitive method for detecting refrigerant leaks and are particularly effective at pinpointing the precise location of small or hard-to-find leaks. They must be recalibrated per the manufacturer's instructions to maintain accuracy. Bubble tests are least sensitive, halide torches only detect chlorinated refrigerants, and dye requires UV light and prior dye injection. See 40 CFR §82.154 and EPA 608 technician study guide.

question: A technician injects fluorescent dye into a refrigerant system to help locate a suspected leak. What additional tool is required to detect the dye?
options: An infrared thermometer
An electronic leak detector
A UV (ultraviolet) light
A combustible gas detector
answer_text: A UV (ultraviolet) light
explanation: Fluorescent leak detection dye must be viewed under ultraviolet (UV) light to be visible. When dye escapes with leaking refrigerant, it leaves a glowing residue at the leak site that is revealed when illuminated with a UV lamp or flashlight. This method is particularly useful for finding intermittent leaks and the dye remains in the system for future inspections. Per 40 CFR §82.154 and EPA 608 technician study guide.

question: Which leak detection method is the simplest and least expensive, but is only effective for detecting large (gross) leaks?
options: Electronic leak detector
Halide torch
Bubble test using soap solution
Fluorescent dye with UV light
answer_text: Bubble test using soap solution
explanation: The bubble test (applying soap solution to suspected leak points and watching for bubbles) is the simplest and cheapest leak detection method. However, it can only detect relatively large (gross) leaks that produce enough flow to form visible bubbles. It is not suitable for detecting small leaks or leaks in hidden, inaccessible areas. Electronic detectors are far more sensitive for small leaks. See 40 CFR §82.154.

question: A halide torch leak detector changes the color of its flame from blue to green when refrigerant is detected. This method is ONLY effective for which category of refrigerants?
options: HFC refrigerants such as R-410A and R-134a
HFO refrigerants such as R-1234yf
Chlorine-bearing refrigerants such as CFCs and HCFCs
Any refrigerant, because all refrigerants affect the flame color
answer_text: Chlorine-bearing refrigerants such as CFCs and HCFCs
explanation: The halide torch works by detecting chlorine in the refrigerant — the copper element in the torch reacts with chlorine to produce a green flame. This means it only works with chlorinated refrigerants (CFCs like R-11 and R-12, and HCFCs like R-22). It will NOT detect HFCs (e.g., R-410A, R-134a) or HFOs (e.g., R-1234yf) because these refrigerants contain no chlorine. The halide torch is considered an older, largely obsolete method. See 40 CFR §82.154.

question: Under EPA regulations, which of the following is PROHIBITED when pressure-testing a refrigeration system for leaks?
options: Using nitrogen to pressurize the system
Using a mixture of nitrogen and a trace amount of refrigerant
Using pure refrigerant as the pressurizing gas
Using an inert gas at the manufacturer's specified test pressure
answer_text: Using pure refrigerant as the pressurizing gas
explanation: EPA regulations under 40 CFR §82.154 prohibit using pure refrigerant as a pressurizing agent for leak testing, because doing so results in the release of refrigerant into the atmosphere when the test is complete or if a leak exists. The approved method is to use dry nitrogen (or a nitrogen/trace-refrigerant mixture) for pressure testing. This minimizes environmental release of ozone-depleting or greenhouse gas refrigerants.

question: A technician uses fluorescent dye to locate a refrigerant leak. After the repair is confirmed, what happens to the dye that was added to the system?
options: It is automatically filtered out by the system's drier
It breaks down and converts to oil within 24 hours of operation
It remains in the system and can be used for future leak inspections
It must be flushed out before the system is returned to service
answer_text: It remains in the system and can be used for future leak inspections
explanation: Fluorescent dye is designed to remain circulating in the refrigeration system indefinitely. This is actually a benefit: if a new leak develops in the future, the technician can use a UV light to detect dye traces at the leak site without re-injecting dye. The dye is compatible with the system's refrigerant and lubricant and does not need to be removed after the repair. Per EPA 608 technician study guide and 40 CFR §82.154.

question: An electronic leak detector begins giving false readings and inconsistent results in the field. What should the technician do before continuing to use it?
options: Replace the detector with a halide torch, which requires no calibration
Recalibrate the detector according to the manufacturer's instructions
Use the bubble test exclusively until the detector is serviced at a repair facility
Reset the detector by removing and reinstalling the batteries
answer_text: Recalibrate the detector according to the manufacturer's instructions
explanation: Electronic leak detectors must be recalibrated per the manufacturer's specifications to maintain accuracy and reliability. Sensors in electronic detectors can drift over time, especially with heavy use or after exposure to high refrigerant concentrations. The EPA 608 technician study guide and equipment manufacturers specify that recalibration is required when results become inconsistent. Substituting a halide torch is not appropriate for HFC or HFO systems, and a bubble test cannot detect small leaks.

question: A technician suspects a very small, intermittent refrigerant leak on an R-410A system inside a mechanical room. Which combination of detection methods would provide both sensitivity and the ability to confirm the exact leak location over time?
options: Halide torch first, then bubble test for confirmation
Electronic leak detector to locate the area, then fluorescent dye to confirm the exact site during future inspections
Bubble test first, then halide torch to identify the refrigerant type
Fluorescent dye only, with weekly UV light inspections until the leak is found
answer_text: Electronic leak detector to locate the area, then fluorescent dye to confirm the exact site during future inspections
explanation: For a small, intermittent leak on an HFC system (R-410A), the halide torch is useless (no chlorine). An electronic leak detector provides the best sensitivity for initial detection and general leak location. Adding fluorescent dye to the system then allows future UV light inspections to pinpoint the exact leak location even if the leak is only intermittent, since the dye will accumulate and stain at the leak point. This layered approach is endorsed by the EPA 608 technician study guide and 40 CFR §82.154.

question: Which type of lubricating oil is required in systems using R-410A or other HFC refrigerants?
options: Mineral oil (MO)
Alkylbenzene oil (AB)
Polyolester oil (POE)
Polyalkylene glycol oil (PAG)
answer_text: Polyolester oil (POE)
explanation: R-410A and other HFC refrigerants require polyolester (POE) oil. Mineral oil and alkylbenzene oil are not miscible with HFC refrigerants and will separate, causing poor lubrication, compressor damage, and oil logging in the system. POE oil is specifically formulated to remain miscible with HFCs. PAG oil is used in automotive A/C systems but is not standard for commercial HVAC. Per ASHRAE refrigeration handbook and equipment manufacturer specifications, and consistent with 40 CFR Part 82 requirements.

question: Why is mineral oil incompatible with R-410A systems?
options: Mineral oil reacts chemically with R-410A to produce corrosive acids
Mineral oil is not miscible with HFC refrigerants and will separate, causing inadequate lubrication
Mineral oil has too low a viscosity for the higher pressures in R-410A systems
Mineral oil increases the flammability risk of R-410A systems
answer_text: Mineral oil is not miscible with HFC refrigerants and will separate, causing inadequate lubrication
explanation: Mineral oil does not mix (is immiscible) with HFC refrigerants like R-410A. In a refrigeration system, oil must circulate with the refrigerant to lubricate the compressor. When mineral oil is used with HFCs, it separates from the refrigerant and can oil-log heat exchangers, starve the compressor of lubrication, and ultimately cause compressor failure. POE oil, which is miscible with HFCs, is the required lubricant. Per ASHRAE refrigeration handbook and equipment manufacturer specifications.

question: A technician accidentally mixes POE oil with mineral oil in an R-22 system. What is the most likely consequence?
options: No negative effect, since both oils are compatible with HCFC refrigerants
The mixture causes sludge formation, which can clog the system and cause damage
The mineral oil will gradually dissolve the POE oil, restoring normal system operation
The system pressure will rise above safe operating limits due to the oil mixture
answer_text: The mixture causes sludge formation, which can clog the system and cause damage
explanation: Mixing POE oil with mineral oil is known to cause sludge formation. The two oil types are not fully compatible, and their mixture can create deposits that clog metering devices, oil separators, and other components. This can cause serious damage to the refrigeration system. When converting a system from R-22 (which may use mineral oil or alkylbenzene) to an HFC refrigerant requiring POE, the system must be thoroughly flushed to remove the old oil before POE is added. Per ASHRAE refrigeration handbook.

question: A technician is retrofitting an older R-22 system to use an HFC drop-in replacement. Which oil change procedure is required?
options: No oil change is needed because R-22 drop-in replacements are designed to work with mineral oil
The mineral oil must be removed and replaced with POE oil to ensure miscibility with the HFC refrigerant
Add POE oil directly on top of the existing mineral oil, as they are compatible in small quantities
Replace the mineral oil with alkylbenzene oil, which is compatible with all refrigerant types
answer_text: The mineral oil must be removed and replaced with POE oil to ensure miscibility with the HFC refrigerant
explanation: When retrofitting an R-22 system to use an HFC replacement refrigerant, the mineral oil (or alkylbenzene oil) must be removed and replaced with POE oil. HFC refrigerants are not miscible with mineral oil; POE oil is required for proper circulation and compressor lubrication. Mixing the oils will cause sludge. Multiple oil flushes may be necessary to reduce the residual mineral oil concentration to an acceptable level (typically below 5%). Per ASHRAE refrigeration handbook and equipment manufacturer specifications.

question: POE oil is described as hygroscopic. What does this mean for a technician handling POE oil during system service?
options: POE oil is flammable and must be stored away from heat sources
POE oil readily absorbs moisture from the atmosphere and must be kept sealed to prevent contamination
POE oil breaks down quickly when exposed to UV light and must be stored in opaque containers
POE oil has a higher viscosity than mineral oil and must be pre-heated before use
answer_text: POE oil readily absorbs moisture from the atmosphere and must be kept sealed to prevent contamination
explanation: POE (polyolester) oil is highly hygroscopic, meaning it readily absorbs water vapor from the air. Even brief exposure to the atmosphere can allow enough moisture to enter the oil to cause problems in the system, including acid formation, ice blockage at the expansion device, and compressor damage. Technicians must minimize the time that POE oil containers and open system ports are exposed to the atmosphere, and must use proper moisture indicators and driers. Per ASHRAE refrigeration handbook and equipment manufacturer specifications.

question: A new R-32 system arrives from the factory. A technician plans to add a small amount of mineral oil to supplement the factory charge. Why is this action incorrect?
options: Mineral oil is too viscous for the high-speed compressors used in R-32 systems
R-32 is an HFC refrigerant and requires POE oil; mineral oil is incompatible and will cause separation and system damage
Adding any supplemental oil voids the manufacturer warranty but does not cause system damage
Mineral oil is acceptable in R-32 systems only if the charge is below 5 lbs
answer_text: R-32 is an HFC refrigerant and requires POE oil; mineral oil is incompatible and will cause separation and system damage
explanation: R-32 is a pure HFC refrigerant. Like all HFCs, it requires POE oil for proper miscibility and lubrication. Adding mineral oil to an R-32 system will cause phase separation — the oil will not circulate properly with the refrigerant — leading to compressor lubrication failure and potential oil logging in heat exchangers. There is no charge-size exemption that makes mineral oil acceptable for use with HFC refrigerants. The system should only use the oil type specified by the equipment manufacturer, which for HFC systems is POE. Per ASHRAE refrigeration handbook and 40 CFR Part 82.

question: Which of the following best defines refrigerant recovery as described under EPA Section 608 regulations?
options: Cleaning and purifying used refrigerant to meet new refrigerant standards before returning it to a cylinder
Removing refrigerant from a system and storing it in an external container, not necessarily cleaning or testing it
Removing refrigerant from a system and immediately disposing of it at an EPA-approved facility
Recirculating refrigerant through the system's filter-drier to remove moisture and contaminants
answer_text: Removing refrigerant from a system and storing it in an external container, not necessarily cleaning or testing it
explanation: Under EPA Section 608 (40 CFR §82.154), recovery means removing refrigerant from a system in any condition and storing it in an external container. Recovery does not require that the refrigerant be cleaned, tested, or returned to a particular purity level — that is the definition of reclaim. Recovery simply gets the refrigerant out of the system and into a container to prevent atmospheric release. Recycle (filtering on-site) and reclaim (factory-level purification) are distinct processes with different requirements.

question: Before opening a refrigeration system for repair or disposal, what must a technician do first?
options: Obtain a written work order signed by the equipment owner
Recover the refrigerant from the system into an approved recovery cylinder
Test the refrigerant purity using a refrigerant identifier
Check the ambient temperature to ensure it is above 50°F for efficient recovery
answer_text: Recover the refrigerant from the system into an approved recovery cylinder
explanation: 40 CFR §82.156 requires that refrigerant be recovered from any refrigeration or air conditioning system before the system is opened for service, maintenance, or disposal. Opening a system before recovery would allow refrigerant to escape to the atmosphere, which is prohibited under the Section 608 venting prohibition (40 CFR §82.154). Recovery must be performed with EPA-approved (third-party certified) equipment. The technician must also be certified under Section 608 if the system contains more than 5 lbs of refrigerant.

question: Who is required to hold EPA Section 608 certification when recovering refrigerant from systems with more than 5 lbs of charge?
options: Only the business owner or supervisor overseeing the work
Any person who recovers refrigerant from such systems
Only technicians working on commercial refrigeration equipment
Only technicians working with Class I ozone-depleting substances (CFCs)
answer_text: Any person who recovers refrigerant from such systems
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.161, any person who recovers refrigerant from systems with more than 5 lbs of refrigerant must be certified under EPA Section 608. This applies regardless of the type of refrigerant or the type of equipment (residential, commercial, industrial). Certification is not limited to supervisors or to those working only with CFCs — it is required for all technicians performing recovery on systems above the 5 lb threshold.

question: A technician is servicing a small appliance that contains less than 5 lbs of refrigerant. The technician intentionally vents a small amount during a hose connection. Which statement is correct?
options: This is acceptable because systems under 5 lbs are exempt from all venting prohibitions
This is a violation because technicians must minimize refrigerant releases even from small systems
This is acceptable as long as the refrigerant is an HFC, which is not ozone-depleting
This is acceptable if the technician holds a Type I EPA 608 certification
answer_text: This is a violation because technicians must minimize refrigerant releases even from small systems
explanation: The Section 608 venting prohibition under 40 CFR §82.154 prohibits any person from knowingly venting refrigerant during the service, maintenance, repair, or disposal of refrigeration and air conditioning equipment. While small appliances (under 5 lbs) have reduced recovery requirements compared to larger systems, the general prohibition on intentional venting still applies. Technicians must take steps to minimize releases regardless of system size or refrigerant type.

question: What distinguishes recovery from reclaim under EPA Section 608 regulations?
options: Recovery requires third-party lab analysis; reclaim only requires visual inspection of the refrigerant
Recovery removes refrigerant without necessarily cleaning it; reclaim purifies it to new product standards
Recovery is only performed on CFC systems; reclaim applies to HCFC and HFC systems
Recovery and reclaim are interchangeable terms under current EPA regulations
answer_text: Recovery removes refrigerant without necessarily cleaning it; reclaim purifies it to new product standards
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.154, the three R's are defined separately: Recovery = removing refrigerant from a system and storing it in an external container (no purity requirement). Recycle = cleaning refrigerant on-site by passing it through oil separators and driers (single-pass cleaning). Reclaim = processing refrigerant off-site to meet AHRI 700 purity standards equivalent to new refrigerant. Only reclaimed refrigerant can be resold as refrigerant. These are not interchangeable terms and have distinct regulatory meanings.

question: A technician recovers refrigerant from a system using a non-certified, homemade recovery device. Which regulation does this violate, and what is the consequence?
options: It violates 40 CFR §82.158, which requires recovery equipment to be EPA-approved (third-party certified); using uncertified equipment is a regulatory violation subject to penalties
It violates OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, which governs process safety management; the penalty is a mandatory facility shutdown
It only violates local building codes and does not trigger any federal EPA penalties
It violates 40 CFR §82.161 only if the system charge exceeds 50 lbs; smaller systems have no equipment certification requirement
answer_text: It violates 40 CFR §82.158, which requires recovery equipment to be EPA-approved (third-party certified); using uncertified equipment is a regulatory violation subject to penalties
explanation: 40 CFR §82.158 requires that recovery equipment manufactured after November 15, 1993 be certified by an EPA-approved third-party organization before it may be used in Section 608 compliance activities. Using homemade or non-certified recovery equipment is a federal regulatory violation regardless of system size. Violations of Section 608 regulations can result in civil penalties of up to $69,733 per day per violation (inflation-adjusted 2025 per 40 CFR §82.34). There is no charge-size exemption that permits use of uncertified recovery equipment.

question: Under 40 CFR §82.157(a), what is the maximum allowable annual leak rate for a comfort cooling (air conditioning) system before a repair obligation is triggered?
options: 10% of the full charge per year
20% of the full charge per year
15% of the full charge per year
5% of the full charge per year
answer_text: 10% of the full charge per year
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(a), comfort cooling appliances must be repaired when the annual leak rate exceeds 10% of the full refrigerant charge. This is a stricter threshold than commercial refrigeration (20%) because comfort cooling systems are generally easier to maintain and seal. The annual leak rate is calculated as: (lbs leaked per year ÷ full charge in lbs) × 100.

question: A comfort cooling system has a full charge of 100 lbs. During the past 12 months, 12 lbs of refrigerant was added to maintain the charge. What is the annual leak rate, and does it trigger a repair obligation?
options: 12%; yes, it exceeds the 10% threshold and must be repaired
12%; no, the system is below the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold
10%; no, it is exactly at the limit and no repair is needed
8%; no, it is below the 10% threshold
answer_text: 12%; yes, it exceeds the 10% threshold and must be repaired
explanation: Annual leak rate = (lbs leaked ÷ full charge) × 100 = (12 ÷ 100) × 100 = 12%. Since 12% exceeds the 10% threshold for comfort cooling systems under 40 CFR §82.157(a), a repair obligation is triggered. The technician or owner must repair the leak within 30 days of discovery.

question: How is the annual leak rate calculated for a comfort cooling system under EPA regulations?
options: (lbs leaked per year ÷ full charge in lbs) × 100
(full charge ÷ lbs leaked) × 100
(lbs added per service call ÷ full charge) × 100
(lbs leaked × 12 months) ÷ full charge
answer_text: (lbs leaked per year ÷ full charge in lbs) × 100
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(a), the annual leak rate is expressed as a percentage calculated by dividing the total pounds of refrigerant leaked during a 12-month period by the full system charge in pounds, then multiplying by 100. For example, a 100 lb system that leaked 10 lbs has a 10% annual leak rate — exactly at the comfort cooling threshold.

question: A comfort cooling system has a full charge of 80 lbs. The service log shows that 7 lbs of refrigerant was added over the last year. Does the annual leak rate trigger a repair obligation?
options: No, the annual leak rate is 8.75%, which is below the 10% threshold
Yes, the annual leak rate is 8.75%, which exceeds the 10% threshold
Yes, because any system that requires recharge must be repaired
No, because only systems containing 50 lbs or more are subject to the rule
answer_text: No, the annual leak rate is 8.75%, which is below the 10% threshold
explanation: Annual leak rate = (7 ÷ 80) × 100 = 8.75%. This is below the 10% threshold for comfort cooling systems under 40 CFR §82.157(a), so no repair obligation is triggered at this time. However, technicians should still note the leak and monitor it. Note: as of 2026, this 80 lb system is subject to the rule since it exceeds the new 15 lb charge threshold.

question: Under 40 CFR §82.157(b), how many days does the owner of a comfort cooling or commercial refrigeration system have to repair a refrigerant leak after it is discovered?
options: 30 days
60 days
120 days
15 days
answer_text: 30 days
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(b), the standard repair deadline is 30 days after the leak is discovered for comfort cooling and commercial refrigeration appliances. The 30-day clock starts when the leak is identified, not when it first began. Failure to repair within 30 days without an approved exception constitutes a violation of EPA regulations.

question: An industrial process refrigeration system is discovered to have a leak that exceeds the regulatory threshold. The owner determines that a full repair within 30 days is technically infeasible. What is the maximum extension available under EPA regulations?
options: 120-day extension, provided the owner applies for it
60-day extension, automatically granted
90-day extension, granted by the EPA regional office
No extension is available; repair must be completed in 30 days
answer_text: 120-day extension, provided the owner applies for it
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.157(b)(2), industrial process refrigeration systems are eligible for a 120-day extension beyond the standard 30-day deadline if the owner can demonstrate that repair within 30 days is technically infeasible. This exception applies only to industrial process refrigeration — not comfort cooling or standard commercial refrigeration. The owner must formally apply for this extension.

question: If a comfort cooling system leak cannot be repaired within 30 days, what action must the owner take as required by 40 CFR §82.157?
options: Develop a retrofit or retirement plan within 30 days
Apply for a 120-day industrial process refrigeration extension
Immediately retire the equipment and recover all refrigerant
Continue operating and repair at the next scheduled maintenance
answer_text: Develop a retrofit or retirement plan within 30 days
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(b), if repair of a comfort cooling or commercial refrigeration appliance cannot be completed within 30 days of leak discovery, the owner must develop a retrofit or retirement plan within that same 30-day window. This plan must then be executed, and the full retrofit or replacement must be completed within 1 year per §82.157(c). The 120-day extension is only available for industrial process refrigeration systems.

question: Under 40 CFR §82.157(c), what is the maximum timeframe allowed to complete a retrofit or replacement plan for an appliance with an unrepaired refrigerant leak?
options: 1 year from when the plan was developed
6 months from when the leak was discovered
90 days from leak discovery
2 years with EPA approval
answer_text: 1 year from when the plan was developed
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(c), when an owner cannot repair a leak within 30 days and must instead develop a retrofit or retirement plan, that plan must be fully executed within 1 year. This provides a structured path to compliance for complex systems where immediate repair is not feasible, while ensuring equipment is ultimately brought into compliance or retired.

question: A supermarket refrigeration system (commercial refrigeration) is found leaking above the 20% threshold. What is the standard repair deadline under EPA regulations?
options: 30 days after the leak is discovered
120 days after the leak is discovered
60 days after the leak is discovered
Immediately upon discovery
answer_text: 30 days after the leak is discovered
explanation: The standard 30-day repair deadline under 40 CFR §82.157(b) applies to both comfort cooling and commercial refrigeration appliances. Supermarket refrigeration systems are commercial refrigeration, not industrial process refrigeration, so they do not qualify for the 120-day extension. The repair must be completed within 30 days of leak discovery.

question: Which type of refrigeration system is eligible for the 120-day repair extension under 40 CFR §82.157(b)(2)?
options: Industrial process refrigeration only
Both commercial refrigeration and industrial process refrigeration
Comfort cooling systems only
Any system containing more than 50 lbs of refrigerant
answer_text: Industrial process refrigeration only
explanation: The 120-day extension under 40 CFR §82.157(b)(2) is exclusively available for industrial process refrigeration systems where repair within 30 days is technically infeasible. Comfort cooling appliances and commercial refrigeration systems (such as supermarket cases and walk-in coolers) must comply with the standard 30-day repair deadline, or the owner must develop a retrofit/retirement plan within that 30-day period.

question: An industrial process refrigeration system leak is discovered on January 1. The owner applies for and is granted a 120-day extension. By what date must the repair be completed?
options: May 1 (approximately 120 days after January 1)
March 1 (30 days after January 1)
July 1 (180 days after January 1)
January 1 of the following year
answer_text: May 1 (approximately 120 days after January 1)
explanation: Under 40 CFR §82.157(b)(2), the 120-day extension begins from the date the leak is discovered. If the leak was discovered on January 1, the 120-day period extends to approximately May 1 (January = 31 days remaining after Jan 1, February = 28 days, March = 31 days, April = 30 days = 120 days total). This extension is only available for industrial process refrigeration when repair within 30 days is technically infeasible.

question: A comfort cooling system leak is discovered on March 1. The owner cannot complete the repair by the standard deadline. What two things must the owner do under 40 CFR §82.157?
options: Develop a retrofit/retirement plan by March 31 and complete it within 1 year
Apply for a 120-day extension and submit a retrofit plan to the EPA
Immediately recover all refrigerant and shut down the system
Notify the EPA within 5 days and repair within 60 days
answer_text: Develop a retrofit/retirement plan by March 31 and complete it within 1 year
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(b) and §82.157(c), when a comfort cooling system cannot be repaired within 30 days of leak discovery (March 1 + 30 days = March 31), the owner must develop a retrofit or retirement plan within that same 30-day window. Once developed, the plan must be completed within 1 year. The 120-day extension is not available for comfort cooling — only for industrial process refrigeration.

question: What does a pressure-temperature (P-T) chart show for a given refrigerant?
options: The saturation temperature at a given pressure
The superheat required at the evaporator outlet
The maximum operating pressure of the system
The subcooling temperature at the condenser outlet
answer_text: The saturation temperature at a given pressure
explanation: A P-T (Pressure-Temperature) chart shows the relationship between saturation pressure and saturation temperature for a specific refrigerant. At any given pressure, the chart tells you the temperature at which the refrigerant will change phase (boil or condense). Technicians use P-T charts to verify system operation, check for non-condensables, and identify refrigerant type by comparing measured pressure and temperature values.
source_ref: ASHRAE Refrigeration Fundamentals; 40 CFR §82 Technician Guide

question: A manifold gauge reads 100 psig on the high side. What is the equivalent pressure in psia at sea level?
options: 114.7 psia
100 psia
85.3 psia
128.4 psia
answer_text: 114.7 psia
explanation: The conversion from gauge pressure to absolute pressure is: psia = psig + 14.696 (≈14.7 at sea level). Therefore, 100 psig + 14.7 = 114.7 psia. Manifold gauges read gauge pressure (psig), which measures pressure relative to atmospheric pressure. Absolute pressure (psia) measures pressure relative to a perfect vacuum and is used in thermodynamic calculations and P-T chart applications.

question: A technician's manifold gauge reads 0 psig. What is the absolute pressure in psia at sea level?
options: 14.7 psia
0 psia
1 psia
29.92 psia
answer_text: 14.7 psia
explanation: At 0 psig, the gauge reads atmospheric pressure — no pressure difference from the atmosphere. Converting to absolute pressure: psia = psig + 14.696 = 0 + 14.7 = 14.7 psia. This represents standard atmospheric pressure at sea level. A reading of 0 psia would indicate a perfect vacuum, which is physically impossible in practice. This conversion is critical when using P-T charts for thermodynamic calculations.

question: A technician measures a low-side pressure of 68.5 psig on an R-22 system. Using a P-T chart, the saturation temperature at that pressure is approximately 40°F. The suction line temperature measured is 50°F. What does this indicate?
options: 10°F of superheat at the evaporator outlet
10°F of subcooling at the condenser
The system is operating at saturation with no superheat
The refrigerant charge is overcharged
answer_text: 10°F of superheat at the evaporator outlet
explanation: Superheat is the temperature rise of refrigerant vapor above its saturation temperature at a given pressure. Here, the suction line measures 50°F while the P-T chart shows the saturation temperature at 68.5 psig is 40°F. Superheat = Actual temperature − Saturation temperature = 50°F − 40°F = 10°F. The P-T chart is the essential tool for determining saturation temperature to calculate superheat.
source_ref: ASHRAE Refrigeration Fundamentals; 40 CFR §82 Technician Guide

question: At saturation conditions, if heat is added to a refrigerant, what happens to the temperature while the refrigerant changes phase from liquid to vapor?
options: The temperature remains constant while phase change occurs
The temperature increases proportionally with heat added
The temperature decreases as vapor pressure drops
The temperature first rises then drops sharply
answer_text: The temperature remains constant while phase change occurs
explanation: At saturation conditions, a refrigerant exists as a mixture of liquid and vapor. When heat is added during phase change, all the energy goes into converting liquid to vapor (latent heat of vaporization) rather than raising the temperature. The temperature remains constant at the saturation temperature until the phase change is complete. This is why P-T charts are useful — at saturation, pressure and temperature have a fixed relationship for each refrigerant.

question: Why must a technician use the correct refrigerant's P-T chart when diagnosing system pressures?
options: Each refrigerant has a unique pressure-temperature relationship at saturation
All HFC refrigerants share the same P-T relationship
P-T charts are only needed for systems with mixed refrigerants
P-T charts apply only to the high-pressure side of the system
answer_text: Each refrigerant has a unique pressure-temperature relationship at saturation
explanation: Each refrigerant has its own unique thermodynamic properties, meaning the saturation pressure at a given temperature varies significantly between refrigerants. For example, R-22 and R-410A have very different saturation pressures at 40°F. Using the wrong P-T chart would lead to incorrect diagnosis of superheat, subcooling, or charge level. Technicians must always identify the refrigerant before using a P-T chart.
source_ref: ASHRAE Refrigeration Fundamentals; 40 CFR §82 Technician Guide

question: A manifold gauge reads 50 psig at sea level. What is the correct formula to convert this to absolute pressure?
options: psia = psig + 14.696
psia = psig − 14.696
psia = psig × 14.696
psia = psig ÷ 14.696
answer_text: psia = psig + 14.696
explanation: The conversion from gauge pressure to absolute pressure is: psia = psig + 14.696 (rounded to 14.7 at sea level). Gauge pressure (psig) measures pressure relative to atmospheric pressure and reads 0 at atmospheric conditions. Absolute pressure (psia) measures relative to a perfect vacuum. Adding the standard atmospheric pressure (14.696 psi) converts psig to psia. For 50 psig: 50 + 14.696 = 64.696 psia.

question: A technician is working at high altitude where atmospheric pressure is 12.5 psia instead of the standard 14.7 psia. How does this affect the psig-to-psia conversion?
options: psia = psig + 12.5, because local atmospheric pressure replaces the standard 14.7 value
psia = psig + 14.7, because atmospheric pressure does not affect gauge readings
psia = psig − 12.5, because reduced atmosphere lowers absolute pressure
psia = psig × 12.5, because the ratio changes at altitude
answer_text: psia = psig + 12.5, because local atmospheric pressure replaces the standard 14.7 value
explanation: The conversion psia = psig + atmospheric pressure uses local atmospheric pressure, not the standard 14.696 psia value. At high altitudes, atmospheric pressure is lower. If local atmospheric pressure is 12.5 psia, then psia = psig + 12.5. This is important for technicians working at elevation because P-T chart values based on sea-level standard conditions may need adjustment, and vacuum gauges may also read differently.

question: Under 40 CFR §82.157(a), what is the annual leak rate threshold that triggers a repair obligation for commercial refrigeration systems?
options: 20% of the full charge per year
10% of the full charge per year
15% of the full charge per year
30% of the full charge per year
answer_text: 20% of the full charge per year
explanation: Per 40 CFR §82.157(a), commercial refrigeration appliances must be repaired when the annual leak rate exceeds 20% of the full refrigerant charge. This threshold is higher than the 10% threshold for comfort cooling systems because commercial refrigeration equipment (such as supermarket cases and walk-in coolers) typically has more complex piping systems that are harder to make completely leak-free.

question: Why does EPA set a higher leak rate threshold (20%) for commercial refrigeration compared to comfort cooling (10%)?
options: Commercial refrigeration systems have more complex piping that is inherently harder to seal perfectly
Commercial refrigerants are less harmful to the environment than comfort cooling refrigerants
Commercial refrigeration systems are inspected more frequently, making higher rates acceptable
Commercial refrigeration systems contain smaller charges, making percentage leaks less significant
answer_text: Commercial refrigeration systems have more complex piping that is inherently harder to seal perfectly
explanation: The EPA sets a higher 20% annual leak rate threshold for commercial refrigeration under 40 CFR §82.157(a) because these systems — such as supermarket rack systems, reach-in cases, and walk-in coolers — involve extensive piping networks with many joints, fittings, and service connections. This complexity makes achieving zero leakage technically more challenging than in simpler comfort cooling systems, which have fewer connections and shorter refrigerant lines.

question: A supermarket rack refrigeration system contains 200 lbs of R-404A. The service log shows 45 lbs was added over the past year. What is the annual leak rate, and is repair required?
options: 22.5%; yes, it exceeds the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold
22.5%; no, only systems exceeding 30% must be repaired
22.5%; only if the system also fails a leak inspection
45%; yes, but only if the system contains HFC refrigerant
answer_text: 22.5%; yes, it exceeds the 20% commercial refrigeration threshold
explanation: Annual leak rate = (45 ÷ 200) × 100 = 22.5%. This exceeds the 20% threshold for commercial refrigeration appliances under 40 CFR §82.157(a), so a repair obligation is triggered.
